signals.details: Orca should be treated as a strong Class A pressure point for worktree-oriented orchestration and open-source mindshare.
signals.next_action: Run a fresh official-source pass before publishing a public battlecard.
signals.objection: Orca may look more immediately legible as a worktree fleet cockpit.
signals.summary: Orca is positioned as a direct local ADE competitor with visible worktree/fleet pressure.
signals.tachyon_response: Lead with verification-first orchestration: show canonical verifiers, preserved diffs, and intervention burden instead of only cockpit breadth.

signals.details: AgentsRoom creates strong first-impression pressure through a visual multi-agent coordination narrative.
signals.next_action: Capture current screenshots and homepage messaging before using in public sales material.
signals.objection: AgentsRoom may appear more polished for teams evaluating visually.
signals.summary: AgentsRoom pressures Tachyon on visual cockpit positioning.
signals.tachyon_response: Compare visible UX separately from reliability: Tachyon should show benchmark evidence, handoff quality, and reproducible task completion.

signals.details: Kandev can appeal to buyers looking for self-hosted workflow ownership and kanban-like coordination.
signals.next_action: Verify install path and current feature claims from upstream docs before a benchmark run.
signals.objection: Kandev may feel safer to teams that want self-hosted project control.
signals.summary: Kandev creates self-hosted packaging and workflow-control pressure.
signals.tachyon_response: Frame Tachyon around controlled local automation, verifier isolation, and auditable agent work rather than project-management breadth.

signals.details: Augment Code belongs in a Class B lane: enterprise context and budget pressure, not direct local ADE parity.
signals.next_action: Keep Augment out of Class A leaderboards unless setup caveats are explicit.
signals.objection: Augment may dominate enterprise narrative around organizational context.
signals.summary: Augment Code is strategic enterprise pressure rather than a clean Class A local ADE peer.
signals.tachyon_response: Separate enterprise platform comparisons from local ADE benchmarks and emphasize Tachyon's reproducible, repo-local evidence model.

signals.details: Pricing and plan details across the roster need systematic refresh before any public comparison table.
signals.next_action: Add official pricing page observations per product, or record explicit absence when no pricing page is exposed.
signals.objection: Pricing uncertainty can weaken buyer comparisons.
signals.summary: Pricing and packaging require first-class watch records before battlecards are sales-ready.
signals.tachyon_response: Keep pricing unknowns visible and avoid implying free, cheap, or enterprise-only positioning without source-backed evidence.

signals.details: Similarweb/Semrush-style traffic and keyword exports are supported as future imports, but no traffic estimate is currently tracked.
signals.next_action: If a paid tool export becomes available, import product/channel observations as separate traffic, SEO, paid_search, backlink, or share_of_search signals.
signals.objection: Competitors may appear to have stronger demand generation, but the repo lacks comparable digital-market data today.
signals.summary: The repo is ready to store digital-market intelligence, but no paid traffic data is treated as evidence yet.
signals.tachyon_response: Use acquisition intelligence as context only; keep benchmark claims grounded in task artifacts.

signals.details: Official page says Orca is free and open source, MIT licensed, and works with existing Claude Code, Codex, OpenCode, Grok, and other CLI subscriptions.
signals.next_action: Refresh the official page before publishing any pricing table.
signals.objection: Orca can compete aggressively on free/open-source packaging.
signals.summary: Orca positions its ADE as free and open source across macOS, Windows, and Linux.
signals.tachyon_response: Do not compete only on price; compare verifier evidence, intervention burden, and local automation governance.

signals.details: Official page says T3 Code does not resell tokens, has no quota caps, and uses the user's existing Claude Code, Codex, OpenCode, or Cursor credentials.
signals.next_action: Track whether T3 Code introduces paid hosted or enterprise packaging later.
signals.objection: T3 Code may look economically simple for developers who already pay for agent subscriptions.
signals.summary: T3 Code presents itself as free, open source, MIT licensed, and bring-your-own-subscription.
signals.tachyon_response: Separate subscription pass-through from benchmark quality; show Tachyon's reproducible run artifacts and evidence model.

signals.details: FAQ states free up to 3 projects with unlimited agents and Pro at $9.99/month for unlimited projects; the same page also says AgentsRoom never charges, so this remains a watch signal.
signals.next_action: Revisit the official pricing/FAQ page and terms before sales use.
signals.objection: AgentsRoom can claim a low-friction free entry point and low Pro price.
signals.summary: AgentsRoom advertises a free tier and a Pro plan, but current page copy should be rechecked before public pricing claims.
signals.tachyon_response: Use AgentsRoom pricing as a sourced caveat, not a normalized comparison, until the pricing copy is reconciled.

signals.details: Business includes $100/month usage across LLM, Context Engine and compute, CLI access, MCP/native tools, top-ups, SOC 2 Type II, and no AI training; Enterprise is custom.
signals.next_action: Track Augment docs for token-based service fee and compute-rate changes.
signals.objection: Augment can anchor enterprise buyers with explicit team pricing and compliance language.
signals.summary: Augment Code publishes a Business plan at $100/month flat for up to 50 seats plus Enterprise custom pricing.
signals.tachyon_response: Treat Augment as Class B enterprise pressure; compare pricing separately from local ADE benchmark leaderboards.

signals.details: Official pricing section lists Free at $0 forever with limits, Pro at $99/year with unlimited projects/agents/sub-agents, and BYO subscriptions/API keys; terms describe annual Pro licensing and two machine activations.
signals.next_action: Refresh both homepage pricing and terms before public pricing table publication.
signals.objection: HiveTerm has clear low-cost packaging and explicit free limits.
signals.summary: HiveTerm publishes a Free tier and a Pro plan at $99/year with a 14-day trial.
signals.tachyon_response: Compare HiveTerm's price clarity with Tachyon's evidence and run reproducibility; do not treat low price as benchmark quality.

signals.details: Official page says Hive asks for no account or key, CLIs bring their own logins, binds to 127.0.0.1, code stays home, and the project uses BSL 1.1.
signals.next_action: Monitor whether Hive adds hosted, mobile, or commercial paid packaging around remote access.
signals.objection: Hive can appeal to developers who want local execution and no separate product account.
signals.summary: Hive is packaged as a local browser-based agent orchestrator with source-visible install path and BYO CLI logins.
signals.tachyon_response: Track Hive as source-available local packaging pressure; compare governance, verification, and benchmark artifacts rather than assuming price parity.

signals.details: Official page describes OpenADE as an open-source ADE powered by Claude Code and Codex, with download for free across macOS, Linux, and Windows.
signals.next_action: Track whether OpenADE introduces paid remote/mobile/hosted features later.
signals.objection: OpenADE can compete on trust, local/offline packaging, and a free download.
signals.summary: OpenADE presents itself as open source, free to download, no login/cloud, and 100% local/offline.
signals.tachyon_response: Answer with Tachyon's own local trust model plus benchmark evidence, not a feature-count claim.

signals.details: Repository README/about text says multi-provider, self-hostable, no telemetry; license is AGPL-3.0. No commercial pricing page was found in this pass.
signals.next_action: Check for hosted/support/commercial packaging before any public buyer-facing pricing table.
signals.objection: Kandev can appeal to teams prioritizing self-hosting and open-source governance.
signals.summary: Kandev's upstream repository describes it as open source, self-hostable, no telemetry, and AGPL-3.0 licensed.
signals.tachyon_response: Compare self-hosting with Tachyon's controlled local automation and verifier isolation; keep license and support model explicit.

signals.details: Official site and GitHub position Fusion as a software factory where agents plan, build, review, and ship code across isolated worktrees, with multi-node mesh, agent companies, and Command Center observability. Treat as direct local ADE pressure similar to Orca/Hive/Kandev.
signals.next_action: Install Fusion, run T001/T003/T005 manual protocol, and capture version plus auto-merge policy before any public comparison.
signals.objection: Fusion may look more complete as an autonomous software factory with quality gates, missions, and multi-device control.
signals.summary: Fusion (Runfusion) is a Class A open-source multi-agent software factory ADE with worktree isolation and plan/review gates.
signals.tachyon_response: Lead with verification-first evidence: identical tasks, intervention burden, preserved diffs, and task-owned verifiers rather than feature-surface breadth.

signals.details: Distribution is open-source and multi-surface: npx/npm CLI, desktop Electron app, mobile Capacitor apps, and local daemon dashboard. Pricing pressure is BYO model rather than SaaS seat packaging.
signals.next_action: Track npm/GitHub release cadence and keep packaging claims refreshed after each minor train.
signals.objection: Teams may prefer Fusion's free MIT install path and multi-device control plane over less packaged local ADEs.
signals.summary: Fusion ships MIT self-hosted packaging via npm, Homebrew, Electron, Capacitor, and Docker with BYO models.
signals.tachyon_response: Compete on reproducibility and trust of agent output, not install friction alone; publish install-to-verified-change evidence.

signals.details: Official site/docs position Maestri as a spatial canvas and orchestration layer (not an agent) with PTY agent connections, APFS floors, routines, portals, SSH, and Ombro. Treat as direct ADE pressure near Orca/Herdr/AgentsRoom with macOS-only parity caveats.
signals.next_action: Optional Mac-hosted T001 smoke with workspace dir = harness worktree; record guest agent separately.
signals.objection: Maestri may look more polished for multi-agent spatial orchestration on Mac.
signals.summary: Maestri is a Class A macOS-native multi-agent orchestration canvas around BYO coding CLIs.
signals.tachyon_response: Separate canvas UX from verification-first delivery: show identical tasks, worktree evidence, and intervention burden.
